title: Asthma Management Guide: ICS and LABAs

points: Effectiveness: Most effective for long-term control of persistent asthma. Target lung inflammation when used daily.
Usage: Daily use is crucial, even without symptoms, to prevent attacks.
Dosing: Start at the lowest effective dose. Gradually reduce once asthma is well-controlled.

Side Effects: Common: Mouth irritation/thrush (prevent by rinsing mouth after use). Growth: May slightly slow growth in children, but benefits usually outweigh risks. Other: Generally safe; benefits outweigh risks when used correctly.
Safety: Safe for pregnant women; not addictive; not the same as anabolic steroids.
Combination Therapy: MUST be used *only* with ICS. LABAs enhance ICS effects but carry risks when used alone.
Benefits: Improve lung function, reduce symptoms, decrease exacerbations.

Risks: Black Box Warning: FDA warns due to increased risk of severe asthma exacerbations (e.g., salmeterol trial showed higher mortality). Dosage Limit: Daily use should not exceed 100 mcg salmeterol or 24 mcg formoterol. Acute Symptoms: NOT recommended for treating sudden attacks.
Usage: Cannot be used as a standalone controller.
ICS First: ICS are the cornerstone of asthma control. Use them daily at the lowest effective dose.
LABA + ICS: Add LABAs *only* when low-dose ICS is insufficient and strictly combine them.

Patient Education: Emphasize daily ICS use, proper inhaler technique, rinsing after use, and understanding the difference between controllers and relievers. Address misconceptions (e.g., ICS safety, growth).
Monitor: Regularly assess asthma control and adjust medication as needed.
This guide aligns with the NHLBI (EPR- (EPR-3) asthma management guidelines, emphasizing step-wise therapy starting with ICS.
